sanskrit: प्रयाणकाले मनसाऽचलेनभक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव । भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक्स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ॥
hindiTranslation: वह भक्तियुक्त मनुष्य अन्तसमयमें अचल मनसे और योगबलके द्वारा भृकुटीके मध्यमें प्राणोंको अच्छी तरहसे प्रविष्ट करके शरीर छोड़नेपर उस परम दिव्य पुरुषको ही प्राप्त होता है ॥
englishTranslation: At the time of departure, one who leaves the body with an unmoving mind, devotion, and the power of yoga, placing the life-force between the eyebrows, reaches the divine supreme person.
essence: Steady devotion carries awareness beyond the body's final threshold.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: At death, a devoted person who keeps the mind steady and directs the life-force with yogic focus reaches the divine supreme person.
simpleInsight: The final moment is not chaos when the mind has been trained to stay still.
detailedMeaning: On the battlefield of Kurukshetra, Krishna is answering Arjuna’s fear of death and the final moment. After describing the supreme, imperishable goal, he now explains the inner condition that carries a person there: steady mind, devotion, and concentrated yoga at departure.
modernRelevance: A surgeon says, 'We need a decision now,' and your chest tightens. This verse points to the kind of inner steadiness that lets a person meet the last, hardest moment without panic.
translationLiteral: प्रयाणकाले (at the time of departure) / मनसा (with the mind) / अचलेन (unmoving) / भक्त्या (with devotion) / युक्तः (joined) / योगबलेन (by the power of yoga) / च (and) / एव (indeed) / भ्रुवोः (of the two eyebrows) / मध्ये (in the middle) / प्राणम् (the life-force) / आवेश्य (placing) / सम्यक् (properly) / सः (that one) / तम् (that) / परम् (supreme) / पुरुषम् (person) / उपैति (reaches) / दिव्यम् (divine)
